---
title: "Instrumental Variables"
subtitle: "Recovering causal effects when treatment is endogenous"
---

## Overview

Difference-in-differences, panel methods, and regression all lean on some version of "the treated and untreated would have been comparable." When that fails --- when the regressor of interest is correlated with the error term because of confounding, reverse causality, or measurement error --- ordinary least squares is not just biased but *inconsistent*: more data cannot save it.

Instrumental variables (IV) offer a way out. An instrument is a source of variation in the treatment that is "as good as random" and that affects the outcome *only* through the treatment. If such an instrument exists, we can isolate the part of the treatment that is exogenous and use it to recover a causal effect.

This module builds IV from the ground up: why endogeneity breaks OLS, how two-stage least squares works, what makes an instrument valid, why a weak instrument is dangerous even when valid, and what IV actually identifies when treatment effects are heterogeneous --- the Local Average Treatment Effect.

## Pages in This Module

- [The Endogeneity Problem](endogeneity.qmd) --- Why OLS breaks when a regressor is correlated with the error.
- [Two-Stage Least Squares](two-stage-least-squares.qmd) --- The IV estimator, the first stage, and the reduced form.
- [Relevance and Exclusion](relevance-exclusion.qmd) --- What makes an instrument valid.
- [Weak Instruments](weak-instruments.qmd) --- When a valid instrument is still dangerous.
- [LATE and Compliers](late.qmd) --- What instrumental variables actually identify.
